Chiang Mai Tourism Overview & Overtourism Context
Chiang Mai is Northern Thailand's cultural capital, receiving over 10 million visitors per year. Overtourism is a pressing issue, particularly at temples in the old city and the Sunday Walking Street.
High Season (Nov–Feb): Peak tourist arrivals, cool and dry weather, highest crowd density at all major sites.
Shoulder Season (Mar–Apr): Smoke haze from agricultural burning; Songkran festival in April brings extreme crowds.
Low Season (May–Oct): Rainy season reduces visitors; waterfalls at peak flow; temple visits more comfortable.
Most Crowded Zones: Wat Doi Suthep, Sunday Walking Street, Wat Phra Singh, Night Bazaar, Nimmanhaemin Road.
Quieter Alternatives: Wat Umong, Wat Chiang Man, Mae Kampong Village, Doi Kham, San Kamphaeng Hot Springs.

How to Avoid Crowds in Chiang Mai — Practical Guide
1. Temples in the old city: Arrive before 9 am — most tour buses arrive after 10 am. Golden hour photography bonus.
2. Doi Suthep temple: Go at 15:00–18:00 for cooler air and fewer visitors, or at 7 am before songthaews fill up.
3. Sunday Walking Street: Arrive at 17:00, before the peak crowd builds after 19:00. Streets become shoulder-to-shoulder.
4. Doi Inthanon: Weekday visits reduce summit congestion by ~60%. Start by 7 am to reach the peak in morning mist.
5. Night Bazaar: Early weeknight (Mon–Thu) for comfortable browsing; avoid Fri–Sun evenings.
6. Nimmanhaemin Road: Cafés open at 8 am; crowds peak 12:00–14:00 and 19:00–22:00.

Getting Around Chiang Mai — Transportation Guide
Arriving: Chiang Mai International Airport (CNX) is 15-20 minutes from the old city by Grab or airport taxi.
Old city & Nimman: Walk (30 min), rent a bicycle (50 THB/day), or use Grab (30–60 THB).
Doi Suthep: Shared songthaew from CMU gate (80 THB one-way). Runs every 20 min from 8 am; last return around 5 pm.
Doi Inthanon: Private car or organised tour recommended. 80 km south-west; 1.5–2 hours drive.
Sunday Walking Street: Walk from Tha Phae Gate (25 min); street closes to traffic from 4 pm–midnight.
Night Bazaar: Short Grab ride from old city (20–40 THB) or 20-minute walk from Tha Phae Gate.
Red songthaew (shared taxi): Flag anywhere; 20-40 THB if route matches. Negotiate for exclusive trips.

Chiang Mai Seasonal Tourism Guide
November–February (High Season / Cool Season)
  Temperature: 10–28°C. Best weather of the year.
  Festivals: Yi Peng (Nov), Loy Krathong (Nov), New Year.
  Crowds: Very high — book accommodation early.
  Doi Inthanon: Cold summit mornings; frost possible in Jan.

March–April (Hot Season / Smoke Season)
  Temperature: 20–42°C. Air quality can be poor (PM2.5).
  Songkran (mid-April): Water festival; extreme city crowds.
  Advice: Carry N95 mask; visit outdoor sites in early morning.

May–October (Rainy / Green Season)
  Temperature: 22–33°C. Afternoon showers are common.
  Waterfalls: Mae Ya and others at spectacular peak flow.
  Crowds: Low — best value accommodation and quietest temples.
  Doi Inthanon: Cloud-forest birding season peak.

Chiang Mai Food Guide — Northern Thai Cuisine
Signature dishes:
  Khao Soi: Creamy coconut curry noodle soup — the quintessential Chiang Mai dish. Best at Khao Soi Islam and Khao Soi Khun Yai.
  Gaeng Hung Lay: Slow-cooked Burmese-style pork belly curry, aromatic with ginger and tamarind.
  Nam Prik Noom: Roasted green chilli dip served with crispy pork rinds (kaep moo) and sticky rice.
  Sai Oua: Herb-packed Northern Thai pork sausage with lemongrass and kaffir lime leaf.

Best spots by area:
  Warorot Market: Authentic local breakfast from 6–10 am.
  Nimmanhaemin Road: Specialty coffee, brunch, international.
  Sunday Walking Street: Street food, mango sticky rice.
  Night Bazaar food court: Wide variety, tourist-friendly prices.

Research Background: Agentic RAG for Overtourism Analysis
RQ1: Can Agentic RAG answer overtourism analytical queries accurately?
Traditional chatbots lack grounded spatial and sentiment data. By combining GPS density (GNSS trajectory clustering) with YouTube visitor sentiment, our Agentic RAG system can answer questions like 'Is Wat Phra Singh overcrowded today?' with quantified density scores and real visitor opinions.
The ReAct agent (Yao et al., 2023) reasons step-by-step:
  Thought → Action (tool call) → Observation (tool result) → repeat until a grounded Final Answer is produced.
Available tools: get_hotspot(), get_sentiment(), search_poi(), rag_retrieve().
Evaluation target: ≥ 80% correct answers on tourism Q&A test set.

Research Background: GNSS Context in RAG Retrieval
RQ2: Does adding GNSS density as RAG context improve crowd-level answer accuracy?
Hypothesis: Providing GPS density scores (0–1) alongside sentiment data gives the agent a quantitative spatial signal that sentiment alone cannot provide.
GNSS data pipeline: OSM GPS Traces → Speed filter (>150 km/h removed) → DBSCAN spatial clustering → density score.
Spatial join (GIS): Sentiment scores are linked to GPS hotspot clusters by proximity (Chiang Mai bounding box: 18.70–18.90°N, 98.90–99.10°E).
Expected outcome: Queries about congestion will achieve higher precision when GNSS density is part of the retrieved context.

Research Background: Token Economics of Agentic RAG
RQ3: How much more does Agentic RAG cost in tokens vs. plain RAG, and is the accuracy gain worth it?
Token cost model (Groq free tier):
  Plain RAG: 1 retrieval + 1 generation call.
  Agentic RAG (ReAct): 1 call per tool invocation + 1 final synthesis call. Avg. 3–4 tool calls per query.
Typical token usage per query:
  Input tokens: 300–600 (system prompt + context)
  Output tokens: 200–400 (chain-of-thought + answer)
  Total: ~600–1,000 tokens / query
Optimisation strategies: prompt compression, result caching for popular POI queries, streaming response to reduce latency.
Groq llama-3.1-8b-instant: effectively $0.00 at free-tier volumes for a 7-day academic project.

GNSS & GIS Methodology Summary
Data source: OSM GPS Traces API (api.openstreetmap.org/api/0.6/trackpoints) + Gaussian-simulated trajectories.
GNSS components used (proposal §2.1):
  GPS Track Points → tourist density per area
  Trajectory analysis → Dwell Time per POI
  Speed filtering (>150 km/h removed) → data quality
GIS techniques applied (proposal §2.2):
  Spatial clustering (DBSCAN, ε=200 m) → hotspot detection
  Bounding box filter → study area 18.7–18.9°N, 98.9–99.1°E
  Geocoding → POI name to GPS coordinate mapping
  Spatial join → linking sentiment scores to GPS clusters
  Interactive map → Folium choropleth + circle markers
Software stack: scikit-learn (DBSCAN), Folium (GIS viz), Streamlit (web app), FAISS (vector search).
